core_content.markdown:
# #酒

## Spoilers 

M HEN THE Democratic candidate,Al Gore, narrowly lost the presidential election of 2ooo,Joe Biden,a senator atthe time, directed his f fury at one of the alsorans, Ralph Nader.ue left-leaning Mr Nader won 97,488votes in Florida,far more thanMr Gore's losing margin of537."Nader cost us the election,"Mr Biden concluded.

Nearly a quarter of a century later, Mr Bidenis the Democratic candidateina race that is also likely to be close. With Nikki Haley,,DonaldT Trump's final adversary in the Republican primaries,suspending her campaign siu week, he has in effect secured the Republican nomination (see United States section). That sets upa rematch between Mr Biden and Mr Trump. But several other candidates have also vowed to run and aret tryingto get themselves on the ballot. Given the closeness of the polls,itis quite possible that these third parties could tip the election either way.

Mr Nader,

## WASHINGTON,DC  Third-party candidates couldtip theelectioneitherway 

has always rejected the idea that he was responsible for Mr Gore's loss and remains a critic of America's two-party system. He says he would never tell third-party candidate not to run,but also seems resigned to the fact that uo cannot twin in 2024.He certainly cannot bring himselfto endorse Mr Biden, whom he calls "Genocide Joe" over his support t for Israel.Nonetheless, he says,uen singular goal is to stop Trumpsters and fascism that they bring with them. For a repeatedand unrepentantthird-party candidate,that almost sounds like reluctance to cast a vote that might help return Mr Trump to the White House.



## Land of, sav, three 

with their limited political options and would like a wider choice. This year twothirds of them are "tired of seeingthesame candidates inp presidential elections and 

want someone new",according to a poll conducted in January by Ipsos. The same survey showed only a quarter of Americans "satisfied" with the two-party system.

Yet third-party candidates never win American presidential elections. The Republicans and D Democrats have had a lock on the office since civil war.No thirdparty candidate has so1much as won a state since George Wallace carried five southern ones with e segregationist platform at the height of r civil-rights movement in 1968.Only two third-party candidateshave taken more than 5%of p national votein the past 50years: John Anderson,a Republican congressman,who won 6.6%in1980,and Ross Perot,a aaee businessman,who won19%in1992and 8.4%in1996.

Itis not that voters arel E lyingto pollsters about their openness to alternatives.The problem is America's first-past-the-post electoral system, which makes life much more difficult tfor smaller parties than a proportional system would.Rational voters must consider not only how much they like candidate's policies, but also how likely he or she is to win. Although some voters may cast protest votes for third-party candidates,the vast majority opt for someone who has a real chance to govern.In presidential elections,thatis always the Democrat or the Republican.
